plainMeaning: As I bow to the spiritual leaders (ācāryas), that salutation becomes the armour guarding my body, and bowing to the teaching-preceptors (upādhyāyas) becomes the firm weapon held in both my hands.
recitation: oṁ namo āyariyāṇaṁ, aṅga-rakṣātiśāyinī. oṁ namo uvajjhāyāṇaṁ, āyudhaṁ hastayor dṛḍham.

wordByWord.gloss: Oṁ — the sacred seed-syllable opening the salutation
wordByWord.etymology: In Jain usage the praṇava oṁ is read as the acronym of the five Parameṣṭhins (a-a-ā-u-m → Arihanta, Asarīrī/Siddha, Ācārya, Upādhyāya, Muni); here it sacralizes the placement of the pada.
wordByWord.notes: Prefixed to each Navkāra line in this stotra; same syllable recurs in the next half-verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit namo from Sanskrit namaḥ, from √nam 'to bow, bend'; the sandhi-form namo before a voiced consonant.

wordByWord.gloss: to the ācāryas (head preceptors of the order)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit āyariya = Sanskrit ācārya (from ācāra 'right conduct' + ya), genitive/dative plural -āṇaṁ; the third Parameṣṭhin.
wordByWord.notes: Third line of the Navkāra; here assigned to the body-armour.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit aṅga 'limb, body'; first member of the compound aṅga-rakṣā.
rakṣā ('guarding', √rakṣ) + atiśāyinī ('surpassing, excelling', from ati- + √śī/śāy, MW 'eminent, exceedingly great'); feminine to agree with the implied aṅga-rakṣā.
wordByWord.notes: aṅga-rakṣā(-)atiśāyinī: the body-guard that excels all others; i.e. 'Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ' is the peerless cuirass.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit āyudha (ā- + √yudh 'to fight'), neuter accusative/nominative singular -aṁ.
sandhi of hastayoḥ (Sanskrit hasta 'hand', dual locative 'in the two hands') + dṛḍham ('firm, strong', √dṛh; neuter agreeing with āyudhaṁ).
wordByWord.notes: Book orthography dṛḍha is written દઢ (without the conjunct); read dṛḍha. hastayoḥ + dṛḍham → hastayor dṛḍham by visarga-sandhi.

literalTranslation: Oṁ, salutation to the ācāryas — (this is) the surpassing body-armour. Oṁ, salutation to the upādhyāyas — (this is) the firm weapon in both hands.
idiomaticTranslation: Oṁ — homage to the ācāryas: let this be the matchless cuirass that guards my body. Oṁ — homage to the upādhyāyas: let this be the unyielding weapon gripped firm in both my hands.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The arming descends one stage further down the warrior's frame. The previous half-verse capped the head (helmet) and shielded the mouth (cloth); now the torso and the hands are equipped. 'Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ' — the salute to the ācāryas — becomes the aṅga-rakṣā, the body-armour, and it is no ordinary cuirass but rakṣātiśāyinī, the guard that 'surpasses' every other. The choice is fitting: the ācārya is the one who upholds and exemplifies right conduct (ācāra) for the whole order, so his pada naturally clothes the trunk, the seat of action and the largest exposed surface. 'Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ' — the salute to the upādhyāyas — is then āyudhaṁ hastayor dṛḍham, the weapon held firm in both hands. Again the placement is exact: the upādhyāya is the teacher who hands the scriptures into the disciple's grasp, so his pada is what the reciter actively wields — the offensive complement to the defensive armour of the line before. Across the two halves the verse pairs protection (armour, passive) with capacity to act (weapon, active), and the feminine atiśāyinī against the neuter dṛḍham marks the two grammatically as distinct pieces of gear. Note the deliberate fidelity of the text: aṅga and rakṣā are kept as the visualized object even as the line glossed is pure Prakrit salutation — the meaning is not in the words 'Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ' themselves but in the act of placing that homage as steel over the body.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the hierarchy of the Pañca-Parameṣṭhin: having honoured the perfected (arihanta, siddha) in v2, it now honours the two living guides of the saṅgha — the ācārya, custodian of conduct, and the upādhyāya, custodian of study. By turning each salutation into armour and weapon, the hymn teaches that reverence (vandanā / namaskāra) is not a passive sentiment but itself a protective karmic act: the merit of bowing to the preceptors literally stands between the soul and harm. The armour/weapon pairing also mirrors the two functions of the path — saṁvara (defensive stopping of inflow) figured as the cuirass, and the active cutting of karma (figured as the weapon) — both grounded in submission to those who embody right conduct and right knowledge.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this line, the practitioner consciously 'puts on' Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ across the chest and 'grips' Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ in both hands — a concrete nyāsa that turns abstract reverence into felt protection. The takeaway for daily life: keep the guidance of one's teachers as both shield (what restrains you from wrong) and tool (what you actively use), so that veneration becomes working equipment, not mere formality.

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Namaskāra (Navkāra) mantra, padas 3–4: Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ, Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ
crossReferences.source: Standard Jain liturgical text; cf. Ṇamōkāra Mantra

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Parameṣṭhi-sajjhāy (on the five Supreme Beings), Devasi pratikramaṇa
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra mantra — the five Parameṣṭhi salutations (ācārya, upādhyāya)
sources.consultedFor: Identification and meaning of the padas 'Namo Āyariyāṇaṁ' (ācārya) and 'Namo Uvajjhāyāṇaṁ' (upādhyāya) embedded in this verse.

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1899), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Glosses and roots of atiśāyin ('surpassing, eminent'), āyudha ('weapon', ā-√yudh), and dṛḍha ('firm, strong').

sources.title: Upādhyāya — the teaching-preceptor in Jain monastic order
sources.consultedFor: Role of the upādhyāya as the imparter of scripture, motivating the verse's 'weapon in the hands' placement.

notes: Verse text preserved verbatim from the book, including the orthography દઢ for dṛḍha (read dṛḍha) and the visarga-sandhi હસ્તયોર્દઢમ્ = hastayor dṛḍham. The framing words (aṅga-rakṣātiśāyinī, āyudhaṁ … dṛḍham) are Sanskrit anuṣṭubh; the glossed padas are Prakrit Navkāra lines, so the 'meaning' lies in the homage-act being placed as armour, not in a literal rendering of namo …āṇaṁ. atiśāyinī is feminine agreeing with the implied (aṅga-)rakṣā; the gendered contrast with neuter dṛḍham distinguishes the two pieces of gear. No independent named composer is asserted (per preface: 'pūrva-sūris').
